sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presents with a history of asthma symptoms, including frequent coughing and wheezing, which she describes as occurring almost every month or every other month for the past six months.
She has a history of exacerbations, including a recent episode one month ago, which required urgent care and a prednisone taper pack.
She uses an albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) as a rescue medication, taking two puffs every four to six hours as needed.
She has a history of hospitalization for asthma symptoms at the age of 8 years.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, with multiple exacerbations requiring urgent care and emergency department visits.
Review of systems: The patient reports no seasonal allergies or other respiratory symptoms.
Current medications: Ventolin (albuterol) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient has diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally, and strong bilateral pulses with brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma, moderate intermittent; Asthma, moderate intermittent (status: active).
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to be taken consistently to prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for albuterol inhaler.
I want the patient to continue using Ventolin (albuterol) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for acute exacerbations.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage symptoms and identify triggers.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in one month, or sooner if symptoms worsen.
